questions.question: According to the 2024 longitudinal study, what is the protective effect of team sport participation on loneliness compared to individual sport?

options.text: Team sport and individual sport both reduce loneliness by about 15%
Team sport reduces loneliness risk by 30% (RR = 0.70); individual sport shows NO protective effect
Individual sport is better for loneliness reduction than team sport
Both types of sport increase loneliness due to time commitment

questions.explanation: This is one of the most distinctive findings for team-based activity. High team sport participation was associated with a 30% reduced risk of loneliness (RR = 0.70, 95% CI 0.53–0.92) compared to non-participation in a longitudinal study. Individual sport produced NO comparable protective effect. If loneliness is your primary concern, team-based collective activity is uniquely protective in a way that solo exercise cannot replicate.

questions.question: What critical mechanistic finding distinguishes team sport benefits from general physical activity benefits?

options.text: Team sports require more physical exertion than individual exercise
Team sports were associated with positive mental health REGARDLESS OF PHYSICAL ACTIVITY VOLUME
Team sports only work if you exercise at least 300 minutes per week
The mental health benefits come entirely from being more physically active

questions.explanation: This is the critical finding that justifies a course focused on the collective/social dimensions. Team sports were associated with positive mental health REGARDLESS OF PHYSICAL ACTIVITY VOLUME — suggesting that social qualities of the team context, not just exercise dose, contribute to the mental health benefit. You can't replicate this by just exercising harder alone. The social context is an active ingredient, not just a bonus.

questions.question: What is the dose-response relationship between team sport participation frequency and resilience in the large cross-sectional study of 67,281 students?

options.text: Frequency doesn't matter — any participation shows the same benefit
OR 1.20 for 1–3x monthly → 1.38 for 1–2x weekly → 1.72 for 3+x weekly
Only daily participation shows benefits; less frequent participation has no effect
More frequent participation actually reduces benefits due to burnout

questions.explanation: The dose-response pattern shows that more frequent participation = greater benefit: OR 1.20 for participating 1–3 times monthly → 1.38 for 1–2 times weekly → 1.72 for 3 or more times weekly. But importantly, even 1-3x monthly shows measurable impact (OR 1.20). You don't need to participate daily to see benefits — consistency over time at whatever frequency you can sustain is what matters.

questions.question: How does the evidence for team sport mental health benefits differ between adults and youth?

options.text: The benefits only apply to youth under 18; adults show no benefits
Adults (18-84) show benefits in recreational contexts; youth show benefits in recreational BUT risks in competitive/elite contexts
Youth show stronger benefits than adults across all sport contexts
There is no age-specific evidence; all studies combine age groups

questions.explanation: The 29-study systematic review focused on adults ages 18-84 and found team sport > individual sport for mental health across the adult lifespan. Youth research shows team sport is protective in RECREATIONAL contexts, BUT youth elite athletes show 16.9% current mental disorders, over half of college athletes report loneliness, and 70-80% dropout by age 15 due to psychological factors in COMPETITIVE contexts. The recreational vs. competitive distinction is especially critical for youth.

questions.question: Based on the research presented, when should someone seek professional mental health support instead of relying solely on team sport or collective activity?

options.text: Never — team sport can replace all mental health treatment
When experiencing severe depression, suicidality, or clinical-level symptoms requiring professional assessment and treatment
Only if you've tried team sport for at least 6 months and it didn't work
Team sport is more effective than therapy, so professional support is unnecessary

questions.explanation: This course provides evidence-based psychoeducation about collective activity benefits — NOT clinical treatment for mental health disorders. If you experience severe depression, suicidality, acute mental health crises, or clinical-level symptoms, you need professional assessment and treatment. Team sport and collective activity can be valuable COMPLEMENTS to professional care (part of a comprehensive approach) — but they're not replacements for clinical treatment when clinical treatment is needed.

title: The Evidence — What Research Shows - Quiz
